ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific term for chrysanthemum milk tea; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) is the closest defensible ancestor class. Chrysanthemum-specific and milk-tea-specific terms are absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific facet for chrysanthemum milk tea exists. Milk/creamer addition and floral infusion are not separately coded in available FoodEx2 facets.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chrysanthemum milk tea or closely equivalent beverage as of 2026.

preparation_method: blended with milk or non-dairy creamer

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of chrysanthemum milk tea is approximately one cup (250 ml / 250 g), as commonly sold at Singaporean hawker centres and bubble tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Chrysanthemum Milk Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for chrysanthemum milk tea (no topping), CR ID D08010018. All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweetened milk tea / sugar-sweetened beverage with milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chrysanthemum milk tea. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened milk tea and sugar-sweetened beverages with milk (GI range 60–70 reported for comparable preparations). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 27.65) / 100 ≈ 18.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 13 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (13 mg/100 g), the high sugar load (11.06 g/100 g) in this beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance on sugar-sweetened beverages; individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this drink and request an unsweetened or reduced-sugar variant.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 11.06 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre in a liquid form causes rapid glucose absorption and acute glycaemic spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chrysanthemum milk tea contains 11.06 g sugar per 100 g with no dietary fibre, delivering approximately 27.7 g of rapidly absorbed sugar per standard 250 ml serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage or request an unsweetened version, as liquid sugar causes faster glycaemic excursions than equivalent solid-food sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from non-dairy creamer (estimated ~2.0 g/100 g) combined with high added sugar (11.06 g/100 g) contributes to atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from non-dairy creamer and high added sugar in this beverage contributes to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption and consider substituting with unsweetened chrysanthemum tea without creamer.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer contributes palm oil-derived saturated fat and potential trans fat, both pro-inflammatory; high sugar with zero fibre provides no anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Non-dairy creamer in this beverage likely contains palm oil-derived saturated fat and potentially hydrogenated oils with trace trans fat, both of which promote systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid this drink or substitute with unsweetened chrysanthemum tea using low-fat dairy milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chrysanthemum milk tea (no topping) is a popular Singaporean floral beverage made by brewing dried chrysanthemum flowers (Chrysanthemum morifolium) in hot water and blending the infusion with milk or non-dairy creamer, then sweetening with sugar. It is commonly sold at hawker centres, kopitiam stalls, and bubble tea shops across Singapore. The drink has a delicate floral aroma, a mildly sweet taste, and a light golden-yellow colour. The texture is smooth and milky, distinguishable from plain chrysanthemum tea by its creamier mouthfeel. This version contains no customisable toppings such as pearls, grass jelly, or aloe vera. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 65 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 2.41 g fat, and 11.06 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with zero dietary fibre and low sodium (13 mg). The high sugar content (11.06 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. The non-dairy creamer component contributes saturated fat and may contain palm oil or partially hydrogenated oils, adding pro-inflammatory potential relevant to rheumatoid arthritis. Chrysanthemum is traditionally valued in Chinese medicine for its cooling properties and antioxidant flavonoids, but these benefits are substantially offset by the added sugar and creamer in the commercial milk tea preparation. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous sweetened milk tea data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 18 per 250 ml serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chrysanthemum milk tea?
Can diabetics drink chrysanthemum milk tea?
Is chrysanthemum milk tea high in sugar?
What is the difference between chrysanthemum tea and chrysanthemum milk tea?
Is chrysanthemum milk tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (CR ID D08010018) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sweetened milk tea beverages, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 2.0 g/100 g based on typical non-dairy creamer composition; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry — this value is an approximation and should be verified against the full HPB nutrient panel when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this beverage as of 2026; no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for chrysanthemum milk tea with non-dairy creamer. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — chrysanthemum milk tea with non-dairy creamer and no toppings contains no measurable dietary fibre. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chrysanthemum milk tea was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. Halal status assumed based on typical hawker centre preparation; individual vendor ingredients should be verified.
